stories.title: New York’s Fair Fares Expansion Is a Win for Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: In a city of straphangers, transportation is about far more than just moving people from Points A to B. It is the thread that ties everything together, connecting New Yorkers to their homes, education, health care, entertainment and nourishment. It is — quite literally — a path to a better future. Affordability is the key to making this complex ecosystem work. When transit is out of reach, both physically and financially, even the most cost-effective living situation can become a trap. Where you can afford to live should never determine whether you can reach opportunity. Yet, for too many New Yorkers, the cost of getting around has become a daily tax on working families. For households already spending half their income on rent, every fare matters. Carlina Rivera. Photo: Courtesy NYSASAF. That is why the historic expansion of the Fair Fares program in the city’s fiscal year 2027 adopted budget is more than just good transit policy. It is a direct investment in housing stability, economic mobility and a fairer New York. This year’s budget agreement added $54 million to the existing $120.6 million Fair Fares program, increasing eligibility for New York City families. Now an individual earning just under $32,000, or a family of four earning under $66,000, qualifies for half-price fares, a permanent increase from 150 percent to 200 percent of the federal poverty level — the largest expansion since the program began. The impact is staggering: 340,000 more New Yorkers will become eligible, raising total eligibility to approximately 1.3 million residents. For a low-income rider, that can mean saving up to $910 a year on transit — money that can go toward rent, food, medicine, child care or utilities. In a city where median asking rent has reached $4,120 — the highest on record since 2010 — those savings are critical. This matters deeply for the affordable housing industry because transportation and housing are deeply entwined. A home is not truly affordable if its resident
bullets: NYC Fair Fares expansion adds $54M to $120.6M program in FY2027 budget
Program increases transit subsidy eligibility for working families already burdened by rent
Transit affordability directly reduces effective housing cost barrier for renters
stories.summary: New York City's FY2027 budget expanded the Fair Fares program by $54 million, raising total funding to $174.6 million and broadening eligibility for low-income riders. The program subsidizes public transit fares for households already spending roughly half their income on rent. By reducing transportation costs, the expansion effectively lowers total housing-plus-mobility expenses, enabling renters to remain stable in affordable units.
stories.impact: For NYC multifamily developers: lower transit costs increase effective affordability and renters' ability to stay in place, reducing turnover. This stabilizes demand in outer-borough and transit-adjacent submarkets (Queens, Bronx, upper Manhattan) where affordability-focused projects compete on proximity to transit. Developers underwriting workforce housing should model transit subsidies as a demand-stabilizing factor that improves lease-up and retention, especially in value-add and ground-up adaptive reuse projects targeting 60–80% AMI tenants.

stories.title: Wholesale Inflation Follows Consumer Prices with June Decline
stories.excerpt: U.S. wholesale inflation followed the example of consumer price inflation in June with a monthly decline. The Producer Price Index for final demand fell by a seasonally adjusted 0.3% in June after rising 1.1% the previous month, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported Wednesday. Analysts had expected the June figure to be in line with the May reading. On an unadjusted basis, the index for final demand increased 5.5% for the 12 months ended in June, representing a slowdown from the 6.5% annual increase reported in May. With food, energy and trade prices stripped out, the index held steady from the May figure of 5.1%. The June decline in the index for final demand can be attributed to prices for final demand goods, which fell 1.4%. At the same time, the index for final demand services moved up 0.2%. As with the Consumer Price Index, a drop in energy prices was a key factor in the June PPI results. Energy costs declined 6.4% for the month after rising 8.4% in May. The report follows Tuesday’s CPI, which showed consumer inflation decelerating to 3.5% year-over-year. The post Wholesale Inflation Follows Consumer Prices with June Decline appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: June PPI fell 0.3% monthly; annual inflation 5.5%, down from 6.5% in May
Energy prices fell 6.4% monthly, largest driver of PPI decline
Core PPI (ex-food, energy, trade) held flat at 5.1% year-over-year
stories.summary: U.S. wholesale inflation declined in June, with the Producer Price Index for final demand falling 0.3% month-over-month and easing to 5.5% year-over-year from 6.5% in May. Energy prices fell 6.4%, offsetting a 0.2% gain in services costs. Core inflation remained sticky at 5.1%, signaling labor and material costs remain elevated despite headline relief.
stories.impact: For multifamily developers: PPI deceleration suggests some relief on materials and goods costs heading into late 2026, but sticky core inflation signals construction labor and skilled trades remain costly. Developers should monitor energy costs (which affect HVAC, MEP efficiency standards) and watch whether the 5.1% core persistence translates to slower wage growth. This is a modest tailwind for project feasibility if labor-cost escalation moderates in 2027, but financing costs remain the primary underwriting driver; PPI alone does not offset cap-rate compression or debt-service headwinds.

stories.title: The Plan: Major League Soccer Scores Big With Global HQ Design at Vornado’s 2 Penn
stories.excerpt: For many people, their love of soccer — or football, as the rest of the world calls it — came from running around the neighborhood as a kid, kicking a ball into a makeshift net while excitedly yelling “goooooooal” with friends. That idea of community and neighborhood connectivity is baked into the newly opened Major League Soccer (MLS) headquarters at Vornado Realty Trust’s 2 Penn Plaza. Actually, the new HQ has that sense of community plus a bright and bold stadium experience unlike any other office environment in Midtown Manhattan. For those two drastically different dynamics to coexist across a two-floor, 126,000-square-foot space, TPG Architecture took inspiration from the game itself. “It all tied into the essence of how we communicated the quality of the sport in architectural language,” said Chelsea Rowe, creative director at TPG Architecture. “With soccer, there’s peaks and crescendos, bursts of activity, moments of flair, moments of greatness, but there are also moments of steady rhythm between that.” When you get off the elevator on the 15th floor of 2 Penn, the immediate feeling is that you have arrived at a major event. It’s a little surprising that “Ya’ll ready for this” isn’t playing at full blast when the doors open. The elevator corridor that goes to the MLS reception area suggests a stadium players tunnel and features the bright red MLS brand above your head as you walk into work. Although, if you have a big marketing meeting with another major brand, that MLS logo at the elevator bank can be changed to match the colors of the corporation you’re meeting — perhaps Caterpillar yellow or Walmart blue. The tunnel leads to an open reception space that features an abundance of light and is infused with technology, including screens showing games, digital scoreboards and a ticker circling the staircase that leads down into the massive communal space called “Midfield.” On display in the reception area is a players’ jersey wall and the MLS Cup, the trophy aw
bullets: MLS leases 126,000 sq ft global HQ across two floors at Vornado's 2 Penn Plaza, Midtown Manhattan
Office conversion signals strong corporate demand for premier trophy assets despite office headwinds
Design emphasizes community and stadium experience, not traditional corporate office model
stories.summary: Major League Soccer opened its 126,000-square-foot global headquarters at 2 Penn Plaza in Midtown Manhattan, a two-floor office designed by TPG Architecture to resonate with sports, community, and stadium experience rather than traditional corporate culture. The lease represents a significant activation of Vornado's trophy office asset in a market where office demand remains selective and pricing has compressed.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact; office fundamentals in Midtown remain weak. However, MLS's branding and presence in 2 Penn signals that select corporate tenants (especially growth-stage and high-margin sectors like sports, tech, media) remain willing to pay premium rents for iconic addresses. This may anchor Midtown's office recovery and indirectly support retail and hospitality in the submarket, potentially driving residential demand for young professionals attracted to the MLS cluster and nearby Hudson Square tech activity. Multifamily developers should monitor whether trophy office and hospitality momentum translates to rental demand in Midtown South and Flatiron submarkets.

stories.title: PTM Partners, Peacock launch subsidy-free workforce housing platform
stories.excerpt: Florida-based PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a platform designed to deliver lower-cost housing without relying on government aid.  The venture targets middle-income renters earning between 80 and 120 percent of the area median income, cutting development costs by handling much of the design, construction and material procurement in-house, according to a news release.  By using a standardized building design and cold-formed steel purchased directly from suppliers, Inception Housing can cut hard construction costs by about 25 percent compared to similar products, PTM Partners CEO Michael Tillman said. The platform’s strategy delivers buildings in six to seven months, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: PTM Partners, Peacock launch Inception Housing; targets 80–120% AMI, cuts hard costs ~25%
Cold-formed steel, standardized design, direct procurement cut construction time 50% (6–7 months vs. traditional)
South Florida needs 30,460 units; Inception underway with Magnolia Flats (88-unit project, Central Florida)
stories.summary: PTM Partners and Peacock Capital launched Inception Housing, a construction-cost and timeline reduction platform targeting middle-income renters (80–120% AMI). By standardizing design, using cold-formed steel, and managing procurement in-house, Inception cuts hard construction costs by ~25% and delivers projects in 6–7 months, half traditional timelines. The company is already developing Magnolia Flats, an 88-unit mixed project in Central Orlando.
stories.impact: For South Florida and Central Florida multifamily markets: Inception's model directly addresses the regional shortage (30,460 units South Florida; 120,850 statewide) by dramatically lowering per-unit cost and time to delivery. Fast construction cycles and cost savings enable developers to build at rents 20–30% below luxury comparable rents without subsidy, capturing supply-constrained middle-income demand. This threatens conventional high-cost development models and may accelerate commoditization of the workforce-housing segment. Developers competing in 80–120% AMI should expect margin pressure and should evaluate modular/standardized design strategies. Live Local Act incentives (land deals, tax benefits) will amplify Inception's competitive advantage in Florida.

stories.title: Developer Wolfe Landau Buys FiDi Redevelopment Site for $35M
stories.excerpt: An entity tied to Brooklyn-based developer Wolfe Landau has snapped up two adjacent Financial District lots poised for redevelopment. The buyer, Landau’s Peninim Water, paid $35 million across two deals for 78 Pearl Street and 46-48 Water Street, according to property records made public Tuesday. The New York Business Journal first reported the deal. The block-through, irregularly-shaped site between Coenties Slip and Hanover Square spans a little over 10,000 square feet. It includes a vacant lot along Pearl Street and an empty, mixed-use building on Water Street. The latter, built in 1929, spans roughly 86,000 square feet. The Pearl and Water street addresses were advertised exclusively by Bob Knakal’s BKREA as “a prime mixed-use development or conversion/enlargement opportunity.” It is unclear, however, what exactly Landau plans to do with the purchase. Spokespeople for Landau’s Watermark Capital Group and BKREA did not respond to a request for comment. Records from the New York City Department of Buildings indicate the Water Street property is being prepared for demolition, the NYBJ reported. The selling entities were the Milstein Organization, commercial real estate firm Swig Company and Weiler Arnow Management. The Milstein Organization, a family-owned firm and operator of Milstein Properties, has owned the pair of properties since the 1970s and `80s, according to public records. The Milstein Organization could not be immediately reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Wolfe Landau's Peninim Water acquires two FiDi sites (78 Pearl, 46-48 Water St.) for $35M total
Block-through 10,000+ sq ft parcel includes vacant lot and 86,000 sq ft 1929 mixed-use building slated for demolition
BKREA marketed as prime mixed-use development or conversion/enlargement opportunity; developer intent unclear
stories.summary: Brooklyn-based developer Wolfe Landau, via entity Peninim Water, purchased two adjacent Financial District properties—78 Pearl Street and 46-48 Water Street—for $35 million. The irregularly-shaped, block-through site spans just over 10,000 sq ft of land and includes a 1929 mixed-use building (86,000 sq ft) marked for demolition. The buyer's plans remain undisclosed; BKREA positioned the site as a mixed-use development or conversion opportunity.
stories.impact: For Manhattan residential development: FiDi remains attractive for ground-up and adaptive reuse residential projects given downtown residential density growth, proximity to the Financial District's working population, and lower land prices vs. Tribeca/Lower East Side. At $35M for a block-through site with ~10K land sf, cost per land sf (~$3,500) is modest by Manhattan standards, signaling Wolfe Landau sees residential upside. Demolition prep and irregular lot shape suggest potential for mixed-use (residential + retail/commercial) or condo conversion. Developers should monitor FiDi zoning and FARs; the site's position near the waterfront may unlock affordability overlays or density bonuses. Expect a residential or mixed-use announcement within 12–18 months.

stories.title: Brookfield in Talks for Stake in Hudson Square Properties
stories.excerpt: Brookfield is in exclusive talks to acquire a stake in Hudson Square Properties in a deal that would value the complex at $3.5 billion, the Wall Street Journal reported. The asset management giant would own 10% of the 13-building, 6.2 million-square-foot portfolio, and would take over as its long-term operating partner. The Hudson Square office district has experienced robust leasing over the past year as rapidly growng tech companies snap up space, reported the WSJ. Artificial-intelligence firms in particular are driving the demand, with Anthropic announcing last week that it had leased an entire 16-story building at 330 Hudson St. If the deal goes through, it would make Brookfield a partner with Trinity Church and Norges Bank Investment Management (NBIM). Trinity Church, which has owned the land on which the portfolio sits since 1705, brought in NBIM to transform what was then a collection of printing facilities into a modern creative hub. Pictured: 345 Hudson St., part of the Hudson Square Properties portfolio. The post Brookfield in Talks for Stake in Hudson Square Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Brookfield in exclusive talks to acquire 10% stake in Hudson Square Properties portfolio for $3.5B valuation
13-building, 6.2M sq ft portfolio; Brookfield to become long-term operating partner alongside Trinity Church, Norges Bank
AI firms (Anthropic leased entire 16-story building) drive robust office demand in Hudson Square tech cluster
stories.summary: Brookfield Asset Management is in exclusive negotiations to acquire a 10% stake in Hudson Square Properties, valuing the 13-building, 6.2-million-square-foot portfolio at $3.5 billion. The deal would make Brookfield the long-term operating partner alongside Trinity Church and Norges Bank Investment Management. AI and tech firms, including Anthropic's recent 16-story lease, are driving strong office demand in the Hudson Square district.
stories.impact: For NYC residential markets: Hudson Square's emergence as a tech and AI cluster (Anthropic, other growth-stage firms) concentrates high-wage employment and drives rental demand for young professionals in adjacent and nearby residential markets (Tribeca, Noho, Lower East Side, parts of Brooklyn). Strong office absorption in Hudson Square signals prolonged neighborhood vitality and pricing power. Brookfield's long-term operating partnership and $3.5B valuation suggest institutional confidence in the submarket's office and mixed-use future. Multifamily developers should prioritize sites within 0.25–0.5 miles of Hudson Square office; tenant base will support above-market rents and high retention. This is a proven office-to-residential demand linkage.

stories.title: London Nightclub Operator Buys 675 Hudson Street for $100M
stories.excerpt: After unveiling plans for a rooftop nightclub at the 14th Street Triangle, London-based club venue operator Caprice Holdings has bought the property for $100 million, according to property records made public Tuesday. Robert Cayre’s Aurora Capital Associates sold the five-story building at 675 Hudson Street to Caprice, which plans to alter the 19th century property — if the New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission (LPC) signs off on the work — and turn it into the New York location for a private club known in London as Annabel’s. The buyer’s address on the deed in property records is tied to Caprice, which is a sister brand of Annabel’s. Both brands were previously under British businessman Richard Caring‘s hospitality empire, but were acquired by Abu Dhabi-based firm DIAFA earlier this year. Caprice secured $165 million in financing from HSBC for its new purchase, according to property records. Aurora acquired the Meatpacking District property in a 2023 bargain sale for $50 million, a deed transfer indicates. It’s unclear who brokered the sale. Aurora and Caprice did not immediately respond to requests for comment. A previous LPC hearing saw agency officials express reservations about the rooftop addition to the landmarked Hudson Street property, stating that the proposed curved glass structure designed by BKSK Architects did not match the character of the building. Comments in the margins of the latest presentation to the LPC recommend that the new owners “improve [the] design relationship between rooftop addition and underlying historic building.” Other comments noted that a “curvilinear form of rooftop addition is fighting geometry of historic building; scale should be reduced.” 675 Hudson is also known as the Herring Building. Construction began in 1849 with further additions throughout the next half century, and it originally served as a factory making masonry supplies. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com
bullets: London nightclub operator Caprice Holdings buys 675 Hudson St. (Meatpacking) for $100M; plans private club Annabel's
HSBC provided $165M construction financing; Aurora Capital sold for $100M after $50M 2023 bargain-sale purchase
LPC approval required for 19th-century property alterations and conversion to private venue
stories.summary: Caprice Holdings, a London-based club operator (owned by Abu Dhabi's DIAFA as of 2024), purchased 675 Hudson Street in the Meatpacking District for $100 million and plans to convert the five-story, 19th-century property into Annabel's, a private members' club. HSBC financed the deal with $165 million in construction debt. NYC Landmarks Preservation Commission approval is required for any alterations.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. However, the $100M price for a single 19th-century Meatpacking property (acquired by Aurora for $50M in 2023) signals continued high valuations for hospitality, food, and beverage assets in the district despite office softness. Caprice's decision to invest $165M in debt financing for a private club conversion suggests confidence in the Meatpacking's positioning as a high-margin, experiential destination. For multifamily developers: continued hospitality and experiential-retail investment in Meatpacking/Chelsea will sustain neighborhood amenities and tourist/night-time economy, supporting rental demand. However, rising hospitality rents may displace other retail and limit diversity; monitor zoning shifts that favor hotels/clubs over mixed-use or residential.

stories.title: Garden-Style Apartments Go for $78M in North County San Diego
stories.excerpt: CBRE facilitated the $77.5-million sale of Sunterra, a 240-unit multifamily community located at 3851 Sherbourne Dr. in Oceanside. The firm’s Rachel Parsons, Derrek Ostrzyzek, Mike Murphy and Kenji Thomas advised the seller, 29th Street Capital. Debt and Structured Finance support was provided by James Flinn and Justin Fitchett.  Sunterra is a low-density, garden-style apartment community situated on approximately 14.24 acres in North County San Diego. Built in 1975, the property comprises 240 units, all configured as two-bedroom and each with a detached garage. It features a range of amenities, including a resort-style pool, fitness center, landscaped courtyards and outdoor gathering areas. Approximately 70% of the units have been renovated.  “Sunterra attracted strong investor interest due to its scale, coastal location,” said Parsons. “With a significant portion of units already renovated, the property offers an opportunity in a supply-constrained North County San Diego market that continues to benefit from strong fundamentals and sustained renter demand.”  If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Garden-Style Apartments Go for $78M in North County San Diego appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE brokered $77.5M sale of Sunterra, 240-unit garden-style apartment in Oceanside, North County San Diego
Built 1975, 14.24 acres, 70% units renovated; strong investor demand due to coastal location and supply constraints
North County San Diego supply-constrained market with sustained renter demand fundamentals
stories.summary: CBRE sold Sunterra, a 240-unit garden-style apartment community in Oceanside, North County San Diego, for $77.5 million. Built in 1975 on 14.24 acres, the property features mostly two-bedroom units with detached garages; 70% have been renovated. The sale reflects strong investor interest driven by the property's scale, coastal location, and the region's supply-constrained fundamentals and sustained renter demand.
stories.impact: For North County San Diego multifamily market: the $77.5M sale price for a 1975 garden-style property (avg. ~$323K/unit) signals robust cap rates and strong buyer appetite for stabilized, renovated assets in supply-constrained coastal markets. Sunterra's 70% renovation completion de-risks the property and attracts institutional money. For developers: North County remains supply-constrained and under-built vs. demand; ground-up and value-add projects targeting 150–300 units on 10–15 acres command premium pricing. New construction feasibility improves if land costs are moderate. Monitor rental rate growth in Oceanside/Carlsbad submarkets; high investor demand and limited new supply suggest sustained rent growth 3–4 years ahead. Coastal-adjacent North County is a prime acquisition target for REITs and value-add sponsors.

stories.title: PGIM Hires David Blum as Managing Director of Commercial Real Estate Credit
stories.excerpt: One of the biggest players in asset management has a new executive overseeing its real estate credit operations. PGIM — the $217 billion global asset management powerhouse — announced Tuesday that it has hired David Blum as managing director, where he will oversee high-yield credit investments into U.S. commercial real estate. Blum takes control of the debt platform of the world’s second-largest real estate investment management firm. He will be based in the firm’s New York office. Bryan McDonnell, head of real estate credit strategies at PGIM, praised Blum’s strength in the high-yield segment of the credit market and noted in a statement that CRE borrowers are looking for adaptable lending solutions, while investors are seeking value-driven debt opportunities — both of which Blum has deep experience in serving. “David’s focus on higher-yielding real estate credit directly supports that need, further strengthening our ability to deliver flexible solutions in an area where we’re already active and seeing strong client demand,” he said. John Jacobs, head of structured lending for PGIM’s real estate investment group, noted in his own statement that the firm has grown its real estate debt strategies division in the U.S. to $9 billion assets under management, and that Blum’s nearly three decades in the industry will deepen the firm’s foothold in that part of the market. “Bringing on David is about continuing that growth, deepening our capabilities, and providing more solutions to borrowers and investors across the market,” he said. Blum joins from Prospect Ridge, where he spent the last four years as a managing director with a specialization in CRE debt strategies. He previously worked in credit markets divisions at Angelo Gordon, GTIS Debt Capital Management and LStar Capital. Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: PGIM hires David Blum as MD of Commercial Real Estate Credit; oversees high-yield CRE debt platform
PGIM ($217B AUM) is second-largest RE investment manager; CRE borrowers seek flexible solutions amid market shifts
Blum brings expertise in higher-yielding real estate credit; signals PGIM doubling down on debt investing
stories.summary: PGIM, a $217 billion global asset manager and the world's second-largest real estate investment management firm, hired David Blum as managing director to oversee high-yield credit investments into U.S. commercial real estate. The appointment signals PGIM's commitment to providing flexible, value-driven debt solutions as CRE borrowers seek alternatives amid shifting capital markets and higher rates.
stories.impact: For multifamily financing: PGIM's hire of Blum and focus on high-yield CRE credit indicates strong institutional appetite for real estate debt, particularly in non-agency, flexible-term structures. This is a positive signal for multifamily refinancing and construction financing markets; large capital providers are repositioning for higher-yield debt and are willing to accept subordinated or non-traditional structures. Developers should expect continued availability of bridge loans, mezz debt, and flexible-term construction financing from asset managers seeking 8–12% returns. However, borrowers must offer strong risk mitigants (off-market yields, shorter terms, covenant-light) to access PGIM-type capital. This capital availability should modestly ease refinancing pressure and support new starts in 2026–2027.

stories.title: $45M Loan Finances Construction of Harlem Condo High-Rise
stories.excerpt: SCALE Lending, the debt financing arm of Slate Property Group, has issued a $45-million senior-secured construction loan for a new multifamily project located at 264-272 W. 135th St. in Harlem. The sponsor, Mass Development, will use loan proceeds for the ground up development of an 11-story residential condominium building. The floating rate loan runs for 30 months with two six-month extensions and was arranged by Arrow Real Estate Advisors. The project is set for completion in the summer of 2028. “Harlem is one of the most supply-constrained condo markets in New York City, with no new project of comparable scale or quality delivering in years, and the pipeline remaining effectively empty,” said Martin Nussbaum, co-founder and principal, Slate Property Group. “That level of scarcity creates a rare and compelling opportunity. We are proud to team up with Mass Development and provide the capital that will deliver 72 residences to a neighborhood that is long overdue for new for-sale product.” Where the apartment industry connects. Meet the owners, investors, developers, lenders, brokers, and service providers driving today’s multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Build relationships, uncover opportunities, and stay ahead of what’s next. The post $45M Loan Finances Construction of Harlem Condo High-Rise appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: SCALE Lending (Slate Property Group) finances $45M senior-secured construction loan for 72-unit condo in Harlem
Mass Development sponsors 11-story residential condo at 264–272 W. 135th St.; completion summer 2028
Harlem condo market supply-constrained, no comparable projects delivered in years; pipeline empty
stories.summary: SCALE Lending provided a $45 million senior-secured construction loan for Mass Development's 11-story residential condo project at 264–272 W. 135th Street in Harlem, yielding 72 for-sale units. The floating-rate loan has a 30-month term with two six-month extension options and is slated to complete in summer 2028. Harlem's condo market is supply-constrained, with no comparable new projects delivered recently.
stories.impact: For NYC residential: Harlem's emergence as a condo destination reflects strong rental fundamentals, demographic demand, and a for-sale supply vacuum. The $45M facility (approx. $625K/unit in total construction financing) indicates lenders are confident in Harlem's demand and pricing power. For-sale condo development typically precedes rental; condo absorption and pricing set a floor for rental comps in the same submarket. Slate Property Group's involvement signals that ground-up condo in supply-starved outer-boroughs (Harlem, Inwood, upper Manhattan) can attract construction capital. Multifamily developers should monitor Harlem condo starts and sales; strong condo absorption (expected 2028+) will validate rental demand and may justify ground-up multifamily projects at 75–85 AMI in the same submarkets. This is a proof-point for outer-borough densification and a signal to acquire development sites in Harlem/East Harlem before comps rise post-2028.

stories.title: Related Beal Plans Complete Upgrade, Renovation of Salem Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: Related Beal, Related Affordable, The Commonwealth of Massachusetts and MassHousing announced a complete upgrade and renovation of the Loring Towers affordable housing community built in Salem in 1974. With financing secured, Related will begin a comprehensive, multi-phased renovation of the 250-unit property in August, extending long-term affordability to 2056. The $38-million renovation, expected to be completed by 2028, will include in-unit upgrades and renovations to common areas. The rehabilitation plan also includes installing a basketball court and a new children’s playground through Related Affordable’s long-term partnership with national nonprofit KABOOM! “At Loring Towers, we’re not just renovating apartments and replacing infrastructure – we’re making a long-term commitment to the Salem community and the families who call Loring Towers home,” said Kimberly Sherman Stamler, president of Related Beal. “We are proud to be working in partnership with residents, MassHousing and the Healey Administration to reimagine Loring Towers and to preserve and expand affordability long into the future.” The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post Related Beal Plans Complete Upgrade, Renovation of Salem Affordable Housing appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Related Beal renovates Loring Towers (Salem, MA), 250-unit affordable property; $38M rehab, completed by 2028
Financing secured; affordability extended to 2056; in-unit upgrades, common-area renovations, new children's amenities
Partnership with nonprofit KABOOM! for playground/basketball court; long-term community commitment
stories.summary: Related Beal, Related Affordable, Massachusetts, and MassHousing announced a $38 million comprehensive renovation of Loring Towers, a 250-unit affordable housing community built in Salem in 1974. The multi-phased project, financing secured and starting August 2026, will be completed by 2028 and extends long-term affordability to 2056. Upgrades include in-unit renovations, common-area improvements, and new recreational amenities built in partnership with nonprofit KABOOM!.
stories.impact: For Massachusetts multifamily markets: this deal demonstrates strong capital availability for affordable-housing preservation and renovation, driven by state incentives, extended affordability covenants, and nonprofit partnerships. Related's $38M investment in a 250-unit property (~$152K/unit) for full renovation shows strong lender and investor appetite for well-operated affordable assets with long-term affordability agreements. For developers: Massachusetts communities with aging affordable stock (built 1960s–1980s) are prime targets for renovation capital; partnering with state agencies (MassHousing) and nonprofits unlocks favorable financing terms and operational partnerships. Extended affordability to 2056 (30+ years) is bankable and attracts ESG-focused capital. Developers should prioritize acquisition and renovation of affordable assets with deteriorating physical condition in Massachusetts; supply, regulatory support, and capital are aligned.

stories.title: Dallas apartment pipeline gets a boost with pair of financing deals
stories.excerpt: Two developers of a pair of apartment complexes in the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex recently secured financing for their separate projects.  Endeavor Real Estate locked in financing for their luxury high-rise at 2700 McKinney Avenue named Lucille. Additionally, StreetLights Residential snagged $54 million in equity financing for an apartment tower in Plano, Texas, which will be built in the redevelopment of The Park at Legacy at 6501 Legacy Drive.  Lucille will feature 265 units across 22 stories in Uptown, according to the Dallas Morning News. The development is helmed by Austin-based Endeavor and equity partner Canyon Partners Real Estate. Construction on […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor Real Estate and Canyon Partners finance luxury high-rise Lucille (265 units, 22-story) at 2700 McKinney, Uptown Dallas
StreetLights Residential secures $54M equity for 6501 Legacy Drive (Plano), redevelopment of 107-acre office campus
Both projects target professionals in Dallas-Fort Worth; completion 2028; Uptown and Plano mixed-use drives demand
stories.summary: Two Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily projects secured financing: Endeavor Real Estate and Canyon Partners financed Lucille, a 265-unit luxury high-rise in Uptown Dallas (2700 McKinney), set to start construction mid-2026 and deliver in late 2028. Separately, StreetLights Residential closed $54 million in equity financing for a Plano apartment tower at 6501 Legacy Drive, part of a 107-acre mixed-use redevelopment anchoring office-to-residential conversion. Both target working professionals.
stories.impact: For Dallas-Fort Worth market: dual financing closures signal strong capital availability for luxury multifamily and mixed-use redevelopment. Lucille's Uptown positioning (financial district, young professional demographic) and StreetLights' Legacy Drive site (office campus repurposing) both reflect a structural shift toward urban, mixed-use infill vs. suburban garden apartments. The $54M equity check for StreetLights on a large mixed-use platform suggests institutional confidence in office-to-residential conversion in suburban office parks. For developers: Uptown and suburban office-adjacent sites (Plano, Irving) are prime acquisition and development targets as corporate relocation slows and office converts to residential. Luxury rents in Uptown ($1,800–$2,200) support ground-up newbuild cost; suburban office parks with weak leasing are distressed and available for conversion. Monitor DFW rent growth and office vacancy; sustained office softness will accelerate apartment conversion and new mixed-use pipelines, compressing luxury rents but supporting mid-market and workforce segments.

stories.title: Texas’ top construction permits for the week ending July 14, 2026
stories.excerpt: New Construction Fort Worth’s public hospital John Peter Smith Hospital filed the state’s biggest construction project of the week to build a $300 million central utility plant at 1575 South Main Street on Fort Worth’s South Side. Construction on the 113,000-square-foot facility started at the end of June and is expected to wrap up in May 2028.  Job filing number: TABS2026024842 Houston Independent School District filed plans to build a 216,000-square-foot career and technical center at 9220 Jutland Road in Houston. Construction on the project, which will feature classrooms, labs, a multipurpose center, kitchen and administration spaces, is expected to […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Worth: John Peter Smith Hospital $300M central utility plant project, 113,000 sq ft, June 2026–May 2028
Houston ISD: $216K sq ft career and technical center, 9220 Jutland, March 2027–Sept 2028
Amazon: $98M distribution center, Terrell (Dallas suburb), 1.2M sq ft, August 2026–2027
stories.summary: Texas filed major construction permits for the week ending July 14: Fort Worth's John Peter Smith Hospital launched a $300 million central utility plant (113,000 sq ft) on South Main; Houston ISD filed a 216,000-square-foot career and technical education center in Houston; and Amazon announced a $98 million, 1.2-million-square-foot distribution center in Terrell (Dallas suburb). These projects span healthcare, education, and logistics infrastructure.
stories.impact: For Texas multifamily markets: infrastructure and employment projects signal sustained regional growth momentum. Amazon's Terrell distribution center adds logistics employment and worker inflow in a Dallas suburb, generating demand for workforce and mid-market housing in the Terrell/Arlington/Fort Worth belt. Fort Worth's hospital and education projects (ISD technical center) reinforce Fort Worth's identity as a growing employment hub outside Dallas proper. For developers: monitor Amazon logistics hubs and similar projects; they often precede apartment demand 12–18 months later as workers relocate. Fort Worth residential (especially suburban and commute-friendly sites) should see rental demand acceleration; developer visibility should span 2026–2027. Workforce-focused projects and logistics employment support mid-tier multifamily, not luxury. Avoid high-cost ground-up projects in tertiary suburbs; focus on value-add and land-light optionality.

stories.title: Dolphins out, superyachts in: David Martin’s marina plan at former Seaquarium getting underway
stories.excerpt: The site of the former Miami Seaquarium on Virginia Key is headed for a major redevelopment.  Miami-Dade County and Terra Group’s David Martin want to trade dolphin shows for superyachts with an application to build what could be the largest marina in the county, the Miami Herald reported.  The application, submitted to state regulators, calls for 325 boat slips, about 90 of which could accommodate yachts longer than 80 feet, and a 500-boat dry stack, the outlet said. The plan includes a fuel depot, a network of floating docks that extends halfway across the channel separating Virginia Key from nearby […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group (David Martin) plans 325-boat marina at former Miami Seaquarium, Virginia Key; 90 slips for 80+ ft yachts
Miami-Dade County negotiating ground lease; Martin receives 95% of slip profit; $19,000+ sq ft fuel dock planned
FDEP regulators raise wetlands, water quality, dredging concerns; final lease agreement still negotiating
stories.summary: David Martin's Terra Group submitted an application to redevelop the former Miami Seaquarium (4400 Rickenbacker Causeway) into a 325-slip marina, including ~90 slips for yachts over 80 feet, a 500-boat dry stack, fuel depot, and floating docks. Miami-Dade County owns the land and is negotiating the ground lease; Martin would retain 95% of slip profit. Florida Department of Environmental Protection regulators requested details on dredging, wetlands impacts, and spill protocols.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. However, the Seaquarium redevelopment into a luxury marina signals continued high-end capital investment and affluent-focused development in South Florida, particularly on Virginia Key and the Miami waterfront. Superyacht infrastructure attracts ultra-high-net-worth residents and supports luxury hospitality/retail nearby, but does not directly create residential demand. Indirectly, marinas and waterfront amenities (fishing, dining) support neighborhood cachet and may attract resident-owners to nearby luxury multifamily. For multifamily developers: monitor waterfront Miami and Biscayne Bay luxury residential projects; superyacht and marina development often co-locate with ultra-luxury condos and Airbnb-eligible apartments. However, environmental approvals remain uncertain (FDEP review ongoing); track permitting timelines. If Seaquarium redevelopment stalls, adjacent sites may remain less attractive.

stories.title: TIF funding for the 78 gets key approval for $425M for needed infrastructure
stories.excerpt: The Chicago City Council Committee on Finance approved a deal to fund $425 million of infrastructure at the 78, a mixed-use development that will include a privately-funded new stadium for the Major League Soccer franchise the Chicago Fire.  The deal would repay developer Related Midwest for project-supporting structures using tax increment financing money, including $287 million in TIF money from a nearby district. The next step in the deal will be getting the formal green light from the City Council, who are set to hold a final vote on the agreement on Wednesday, according to Crain’s.  Joe Mansueto, the Fire’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago Finance Committee approves $425M infrastructure funding for 78 mixed-use development (Related Midwest, MLS stadium)
TIF agreement redirects $287M from Canal/Congress TIF district to Roosevelt/Clark district where 78 is located
Final City Council vote set for Wednesday; privately-funded MLS stadium, broader mixed-use vision requires public infrastructure support
stories.summary: Chicago's Finance Committee approved a $425 million infrastructure funding package for the 78, a Related Midwest-led mixed-use development anchored by a privately-funded MLS stadium for the Chicago Fire. The deal redirects $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the Roosevelt/Clark TIF district. The package covers road, transit, and site-level infrastructure; final City Council vote is scheduled for Wednesday.
stories.impact: For Chicago multifamily market: the 78's infrastructure funding unlocks a major mixed-use development and signals public-private partnership willingness to invest in downtown redevelopment. TIF reallocation ($287M from Canal/Congress to Roosevelt/Clark) may slow investment in Union Station and west-loop transit corridors, but accelerates 78 site activation. MLS stadium + mixed-use (residential, retail) will draw young professionals, tourists, and event-goers, supporting nearby apartment demand in West Loop and South Loop. For developers: the 78's approval and funding clarity removes a key risk and likely triggers additional mixed-use and residential starts in Roosevelt/Clark and nearby West Loop. Monitor post-approval site acquisition and phasing timelines; phase 1 residential is likely 2027–2028 delivery. Developers should acquire sites within 0.25 miles of the 78 before land prices rise in response to infrastructure certainty. This is a catalyst for Chicago's central-west corridor residential development.

stories.title: Housing Notes: Developers are in on the joke: Apologize later
stories.excerpt: I have long had a fascination with the grit it takes to be a Manhattan developer. After all, I taught market analysis to a class of approximately 150 graduate students in Columbia’s Master of Science in Real Estate Development (MSRED) program for 8 years. My students have ended up all over the U.S. and hopefully they learned not to accept “no” as an answer when they’re out in the world. That’s the dividing line since developers tend to ignore the word “no” and keep pushing, when non-developers like me would cower in fear and self-doubt. It helps to look at […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Real estate development requires grit and willingness to ignore 'no' — Manhattan developer mindset emphasized
Author taught MSRED at Columbia; highlights developer persistence vs. non-developer risk aversion as dividing line
Article focuses on developer psychology and market approach; limited concrete market data or actionable insights
stories.summary: This article from The Real Deal reflects on the psychology and mindset of Manhattan developers, emphasizing the tenacity required to navigate complex approvals, financing, and regulatory obstacles. The author, who taught real estate development at Columbia, contrasts developer persistence with non-developer caution and uses anecdotes to illustrate how developers push past rejection and doubt to execute projects.
stories.impact: Limited to no direct impact on multifamily demand, supply, capital, or cost metrics. This is a thought-leadership/culture piece, not a market intelligence story. Developers should read for mindset and negotiation strategy insights, but it contains no actionable data on financing availability, zoning changes, labor costs, material prices, or market fundamentals. Not relevant to underwriting or site acquisition decisions.
